categories.name: Property Tax
categories.tldr: When people own homes or buildings, they pay a tax based on how much the property is worth. This is the city's biggest income source.

lineItems.name: Secured Property Tax
lineItems.description: Annual property tax revenue from residential and commercial properties based on assessed valuations under Proposition 13.
lineItems.tldr: Taxes on homes, apartments, and business buildings -- the biggest slice of city income.

lineItems.name: Unsecured Property Tax
lineItems.description: Property tax on business equipment, fixtures, and other personal property not attached to land.
lineItems.tldr: Taxes on business equipment like computers, machinery, and furniture.

lineItems.name: Supplemental Property Tax
lineItems.description: Additional property tax assessed when property changes ownership or new construction is completed.
lineItems.tldr: Extra tax collected when a property gets sold or a new building goes up.

categories.name: Sales Tax
categories.tldr: A small percentage added to things you buy at stores and restaurants. The Cerritos Auto Square generates a huge chunk of this.

lineItems.name: Auto Square Sales Tax
lineItems.description: Sales tax revenue generated by the Cerritos Auto Square, the largest auto mall in the western United States.
lineItems.tldr: Revenue from car sales at the famous Cerritos Auto Square -- a huge money maker for the city.

lineItems.name: Retail & Restaurant Sales Tax
lineItems.description: Sales tax from the Cerritos Towne Center, Los Cerritos Center mall, and other retail and dining establishments.
lineItems.tldr: Sales tax from shopping malls, local restaurants, and stores around the city.

categories.name: Utility User Tax
categories.tldr: A tax on electricity, gas, phone, and cable bills. When your family pays the electric bill, a small part goes to the city.

lineItems.name: Electric & Gas Utility Tax
lineItems.description: Tax on electricity and natural gas consumption billed by Southern California Edison and SoCalGas.
lineItems.tldr: Tax collected from electricity and gas bills paid by homes and businesses.

lineItems.name: Telecommunications Utility Tax
lineItems.description: Tax on telephone, cable television, and internet services billed to Cerritos residents and businesses.
lineItems.tldr: Tax from phone, cable, and internet bills.

categories.name: Fees & Service Charges
categories.tldr: Money the city collects when people use specific services, like building permits, recreation classes, or renting a park picnic area.

lineItems.name: Building Permits & Fees
lineItems.description: Revenue from building permits, plan check fees, and development impact fees for new construction and remodeling projects.
lineItems.tldr: Fees paid by builders and homeowners who want to construct or remodel something.

lineItems.name: Recreation Fees
lineItems.description: Registration fees for recreation classes, sports leagues, camps, facility rentals, and special events.
lineItems.tldr: Money from sports sign-ups, class registrations, and renting park spaces for parties.

lineItems.name: Water Service Fees
lineItems.description: Monthly water service charges, connection fees, and meter installation charges.
lineItems.tldr: What residents and businesses pay on their water bill each month.

categories.name: Intergovernmental Revenue
categories.tldr: Money that comes from the state or federal government -- like grants for road repairs or public safety programs.

lineItems.name: Gas Tax & Road Funds
lineItems.description: State-shared gasoline tax revenues and SB 1 Road Repair and Accountability Act funds designated for street and road maintenance.
lineItems.tldr: State gas tax money that must be spent on fixing and improving city streets.

lineItems.name: State & Federal Grants
lineItems.description: Competitive and formula grants for public safety, community development, parks improvement, and environmental programs.
lineItems.tldr: Grants won from Sacramento and Washington for special projects and improvements.

lineItems.name: Vehicle License Fee
lineItems.description: City's share of the state vehicle license fee (VLF) in-lieu revenue distributed based on population.
lineItems.tldr: A share of the fee people pay when they register their cars with the state.

categories.name: Other Revenue
categories.tldr: A mix of smaller income sources -- like interest the city earns on its savings, fines, and renting out city-owned property.

lineItems.name: Investment Income
lineItems.description: Interest and earnings on city pooled investment funds and treasury deposits managed per the city's investment policy.
lineItems.tldr: Interest the city earns by investing its cash reserves, just like a savings account.

lineItems.name: Fines & Forfeitures
lineItems.description: Revenue from parking citations, code enforcement penalties, vehicle impound fees, and other regulatory fines.
lineItems.tldr: Money collected from parking tickets, code violations, and other fines.

lineItems.name: Property Rental Income
lineItems.description: Lease revenue from city-owned commercial properties, cell tower site agreements, and facility use agreements.
lineItems.tldr: Rent from businesses and cell towers using city-owned land and buildings.
